title: Modernism and Kitsch Are Structural Twins, Not Opposites

source.original: The Post-Christian Condition – The Abrahamic Metacritique (Critique and Digest)

body_html:
The standard narrative of art history positions aesthetic modernism as a heroic revolt against the flood of kitsch unleashed by late Romanticism. Modernism, we are told, stripped away the false sentiment, confronted the void honestly, and made art adequate to the disenchanted modern condition. This story is itself a piece of modernist self-mythology — and it needs to be challenged, because modernism and kitsch share the identical structural problem: the self-referential loop, the gesture that no longer points outside itself, the form that has lost its content and can only make that loss its content.

The difference between them is one of register, not of kind. Kitsch fills the empty form with the feeling of having content — it substitutes the sensation of transcendence for transcendence, the emotion of meaning for meaning, and does so with enthusiasm and escalation. Modernism, by contrast, makes the empty form itself the subject, turning the loss of content into the only remaining content. It is kitsch at a lower temperature. But a husk that knows it is a husk and makes its huskness into art is not less of a husk — it is more completely one.

The consequences play out across every medium. Modernist painting becomes meta-painting, about the surface and the gesture and the act of painting itself. Modernist fiction narrates the impossibility of narration. Modernist cuisine becomes the deconstructed dish, the performance of its own making. In each case, the outside referent has been evacuated and what remains is a lecture about the activity itself, which then becomes both subject and object — a closed loop with no exit.

If kitsch confuses the aesthetic with the ethical by taking feeling as proof of depth, modernism makes the opposite error: it takes the ethical for the aesthetic, turning the acknowledgment of meaninglessness into an artistic program and expecting you to find the lecture enjoyable. This is, at bottom, kitsch's essential operation performed with maximum theoretical sophistication — a highly refined entertainment that tells you nothing means anything and demands your admiration for saying so.

The deeper problem is that modernism's self-awareness, which was supposed to be its advantage over kitsch, has been folded back into the condition and aestheticized. The awareness of emptiness becomes another form of content, another object of consumption, foreclosing the one thing it was supposed to make possible: a genuine reckoning with what is lacking and why. Both kitsch and modernism, in the final analysis, are sealed tombs. One fills the tomb with manufactured feeling. The other curates the emptiness. Neither rolls the stone.
